title: WebAuthn attestation formats — reference data
description: The six attestation formats supported by the WebAuthn spec + this module. Each format identifies the shape of the attestation statement the authenticator emits at registration. The `WebauthnAttestationVerifier` dispatches to a format-specific verifier based on this value. Documented here for reviewer convenience — the actual implementation lives in `web-auth/webauthn-lib`.

formats.name: packed
formats.description: The most common attestation format in the modern ecosystem — used by YubiKeys, Google Titan, and many mobile authenticators. Contains a signature over the authenticator data + client-data-hash, optionally with an X.509 certificate chain.
formats.notes: Packed attestation with `x5c` (certificate chain) is the fullest form. Packed without `x5c` (self attestation) is also valid but provides less information about the authenticator model.

formats.name: fido-u2f
formats.description: Legacy attestation format used by the original U2F protocol (pre-WebAuthn). Uses an ECDSA P-256 signature over the authenticator data + a fixed AAGUID (`00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000`) for all U2F keys.
formats.aaguid_reported: always all-zeros — the U2F protocol predates AAGUID
formats.notes: Backwards-compatible with U2F-only devices. Not emitted by modern (post-2020) authenticators.

formats.name: android-key
formats.description: Attestation format emitted by Android's hardware-backed KeyStore (StrongBox on Pixel 3+ / Samsung Knox / etc.). Uses an ECDSA P-256 signature + Google's Android Attestation extension in the X.509 certificate chain.
formats.certificate_chain_verifiable: yes (chain roots at Google's Attestation Root CA)
formats.notes: The attestation extension can be parsed to reveal whether the key is stored in TEE / StrongBox — used for tenants with stricter hardware requirements.

formats.name: apple
formats.description: Attestation format emitted by Apple's platform authenticators (Touch ID / Face ID / Apple Watch double-click). Uses an ECDSA P-256 signature + Apple's attestation extension.
authenticators: iOS Touch ID / Face ID (Safari + Chrome + Firefox on iOS)
formats.certificate_chain_verifiable: yes (chain roots at Apple's WebAuthn Root CA)
formats.notes: Apple's attestation is 'anonymous' by design — it identifies that the key is genuinely from an Apple device but doesn't identify the individual device.

formats.name: tpm
formats.description: Attestation format emitted by Windows Hello + other TPM-backed authenticators. Uses an RSA / ECDSA signature + the TPM's endorsement key certificate.
formats.certificate_chain_verifiable: yes (chain roots at the TPM vendor's Endorsement CA — Intel / Infineon / NXP / etc.)
formats.notes: TPM attestation carries the most detailed device-model information of any format but also the most complex verification chain.

formats.name: none
formats.description: No attestation. The authenticator emits only the public key + credential ID. Privacy-preserving: does not identify the device model. Recommended by W3C for most consumer applications.
authenticators: synced passkeys (iCloud Keychain / Google Password Manager) — these ALWAYS emit 'none' because syncing across devices makes device attestation meaningless
formats.notes: Our default configuration (`attestation_conveyance='none'`) requests this format from every authenticator — we don't need attestation for consumer accounts. Tenants with FIDO MDS compliance mandates set `attestation_conveyance='direct'` to force a real attestation format.

notes: The `mfa.webauthn.attestation_conveyance` config knob controls which format authenticators emit. Setting it to `none` (our default) is privacy-preserving; setting it to `direct` forces a real attestation for enterprise auditors.

The `aaguid` column on WebauthnCredential stores whatever the authenticator reported at registration — null when attestation_conveyance='none' + the authenticator withheld the AAGUID (allowed by spec for passkeys).
